summary: Cantrill reflects on 2015 as the year containers exploded into mainstream adoption, but notes this success came with a significant downside: overwhelming confusion from too many competing technologies and frameworks. He argues the container ecosystem desperately needs standardization and interoperability, drawing parallels to how the IETF enabled the interoperable internet. He expresses cautious optimism that the newly launched Cloud-native Computing Foundation could serve this clarifying role for container infrastructure.
key_points: 2015 was definitively the year of containers, evidenced by proliferating container conferences and technologies
The impedance mismatch between development and production containers drove massive innovation but also confusion
Industry consensus at KubeCon was that container confusion is still accelerating, not peaking
Technologies should differentiate on implementation quality rather than market appetite
The CNCF could potentially play an IETF-like role in establishing container standards
money_quotes: The flurry of choice can become a blizzard of confusion – especially when and where there is seemingly overlap between two technologies.

Are we at or near Peak Confusion in the container space? The conclusion among everyone I spoke with was that we're nowhere near Peak Confusion – with many even saying that confusion is still accelerating.

Instead of viewing containers as new territory for conquest, we must take it upon ourselves to assure for users an interoperable and composable future – one in which technologies can differentiate themselves based on the qualities of their implementation rather than the voraciousness of their appetite.
Even for those of us who have been in containers for years, this has been a little terrifying – and I can imagine for those entirely new to containers, it's downright paralyzing.
key_insight: The container ecosystem's explosive growth created a crisis of confusion that only industry-wide commitment to interoperability and standards—not competitive conquest—can resolve.
